fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up that record-mapping library we've been discussing. The core idea is that devs are sick of writing the same boilerplate row-mapping and relationship-stitching code over and over — it's causing those N+1 read issues we keep seeing in prod and the error messages are all over the place depending on who wrote the query. We basically need something like what we did with the loader abstraction in the billing module, but more general-purpose.

The library should let you pull back lightweight records with just the fields you care about, handle related data (both direct and the multi-hop stuff), and support batching so we're not blowing up memory on big result sets. Raw SQL should still work for the cases where people need it. Projections to scalar arrays would be a nice-to-have too.

One thing that keeps biting us — when a field wasn't selected or a relationship wasn't loaded, the error that comes back is totally inconsistent. We need stable, predictable errors that don't leak internal stack details.

Also there was some discussion about polymorphic targets (where the related record could come from different tables per row) and indirect traversal paths — both need to work. Output should be deterministic JSON on stdout. Ask me if anything's unclear, but I think the test files cover most of the edge cases.

trigger_keywords: ordered records output format
what does the records array look like
how are basic query results returned
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Basic ordered query results must be returned as a JSON object with a `records` key containing an array of scalar values (e.g., names). For example: {"records":["Bar","Foo"]}. The order must be deterministic (e.g., alphabetical ascending). Selected-field queries use a `rows` key instead, containing an array of objects with only the requested scalar fields.

trigger_keywords: first match behavior
single row retrieval
how does first_account_by_login work
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: First-match queries must include the matched row fields plus a `sql` array showing the query used, specifically including a LIMIT 1 clause in the SQL trace string. If the record is required but not found, the output must be exactly {"error":"not_found"}. The sql trace format is: "root: SELECT <table>.* FROM <table> WHERE <condition> LIMIT 1".

trigger_keywords: raw SQL placeholder styles
percent vs named vs question mark parameters
how to bind array parameters in SQL
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The library must support three placeholder styles for raw SQL: percent-style (%s), named-style (:name), and question-mark positional (?). All three must correctly bind both scalar and array (list) parameters and return the same deterministic record output. Array values must be expanded inline.

trigger_keywords: scalar projection output
project single column vs multiple columns
values key format for projections
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scalar projections return a JSON object with a `values` key. Single-column projections produce a flat array of scalars. Multi-column projections produce an array of row-arrays (e.g., [["Widget A", 8], ...]). Computed expressions like string length are supported and must preserve row order.

trigger_keywords: batching output format
batch_count and batches keys
flattened array in batch results
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Batch iteration with a batch_size returns a JSON object with three keys: `batch_count` (integer), `batches` (array of arrays), and `flattened` (single flat array of all records). Offset/limit-constrained batch requests return only a `records` array. Batching must preserve relationship data loaded on each record.

trigger_keywords: batch with offset and limit
offset and limit interaction with batching
partial result set from batch
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When an offset is provided, the result skips that many records and returns the rest as a flat `records` array. When a limit is provided, only up to that many records are returned as a flat `records` array. Offset and limit can be combined with batch_size but the output key is `records`, not `batches`.

trigger_keywords: direct relationship types supported
many-to-one one-to-one one-to-many join table
how relationship loading works in records
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Direct relationship loading supports: many-to-one (parent group lookup), one-to-one (single detail), one-to-many (child amounts as array), and join-table collections. Each related value is inlined into the record output. For collections, the value is an array of scalars or objects. Relationship data must be pre-loaded, not lazy.

trigger_keywords: indirect relationship traversal plan
none vs select traversal
multi-hop relationship SQL trace
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Indirect relationship loading supports two traversal plans: 'none' (loads all columns from intermediate tables, e.g., SELECT orders.* ...) and 'select' (loads only the key columns needed for traversal, e.g., SELECT id, customer_id FROM orders ...). Both must produce identical final related values. SQL trace lines use the format: "root.through(orders).through(line_items).categories: SELECT ...".

trigger_keywords: filtered indirect relationship
filter on traversal target
load_filtered_indirect_groups behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Filtered indirect relationship loading applies a condition on the final related target. Only matching related records are included per root record. Root records with no matching related records still appear in output but with an empty array for that relationship field.

trigger_keywords: polymorphic relationship target
typed target loading
different source tables per row
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Typed (polymorphic) target loading resolves each row to its correct related record even when different rows reference different source tables (e.g., widgets vs splines). Output records contain the amount and resolved item name. Results must be ordered by amount ascending.

trigger_keywords: error when relationship not loaded
unloaded association error format
association_not_loaded error structure
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Accessing a relationship that was not loaded must produce: {"error":"association_not_loaded","association":"<rel_name>","record":"<ModelName>","trace":"root"}. The trace reflects the query path depth (e.g., "root" for top-level, "root.orders.line_items" for nested).

trigger_keywords: error when field not selected
column_not_selected error format
unselected field access error
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Accessing a column that was not included in a select query must produce: {"error":"column_not_selected","column":"<col_name>","record":"<ModelName>","trace":"<query_path>"}. Nested path errors must show the full dot-separated trace (e.g., "root.orders.line_items").

trigger_keywords: undefined member error on related record
undefined_member error format
accessing nonexistent method on related record
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Accessing a member that does not exist on a related record must produce: {"error":"undefined_member","member":"<member_name>","trace":"<query_path>"}. The trace reflects the dot-path to the related record (e.g., "root.category").

trigger_keywords: boolean casting from database
coded status values
localized timestamp format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Value casting must support: (1) boolean status — integer/string DB values mapped to true/false JSON booleans; (2) coded status — numeric codes mapped to string labels, with unmapped codes returning null; (3) localized timestamps — UTC DB timestamps converted to a specific timezone offset, formatted as ISO 8601 with offset (e.g., "2017-12-29T10:00:37-05:00").

trigger_keywords: key-based field access on records
string vs symbol key access
record equality comparison
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Returned record objects must support both string-key and symbol-key (or equivalent language-idiomatic key type) field access, returning the same value. Record equality must be based on the underlying row identity — two references to the same row must be equal, while records representing different rows must not be equal.

trigger_keywords: delegate model behavior on record
callback or block value in delegated behavior
argument passing to delegated method
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Records must be able to delegate to model-defined behavior. The delegated method must support: (1) no extra argument — returns computed value from loaded fields; (2) with an argument — appends argument to result; (3) with argument and block/callback value — appends both. Example output progression: "Foo - All about Widget A", "Foo - All about Widget A - foo", "Foo - All about Widget A - foo - bar".

trigger_keywords: SQL trace format for indirect loading
query path label in SQL output
IN clause ordering in SQL trace
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: SQL trace arrays must use dot-notation path labels (e.g., "root.through(orders).through(line_items).categories"). The IN clause values must be sorted numerically ascending. All IDs in the IN clause must be comma-space separated with no trailing comma. Trace is only included in output when explicitly requested by the scenario.

trigger_keywords: separation of IO from core logic
stdin stdout adapter vs core engine
how JSON commands map to library calls
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The execution adapter (handling stdin/stdout JSON) must be strictly separated from the core domain logic. The core engine must never reference stdin, stdout, or JSON directly. The adapter translates JSON commands into idiomatic method calls on the core library. This is a hard architectural requirement.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: like what we did with the loader abstraction in the billing module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The relationship loading subsystem described in features 5, 6, and 7 — specifically the direct/indirect/polymorphic association loading logic that pre-fetches related records in batch rather than per-row

context_pointers.vague_reference: the test files cover most of the edge cases
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The JSON files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ (feature1 through feature11) which define the black-box stdin/stdout contract for the execution adapter

context_pointers.vague_reference: compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The first_account_by_login scenario in feature1_relational_queries.json, which covers LIMIT 1 SQL enforcement, required/optional behavior, and the not_found error contract

context_pointers.vague_reference: stable, predictable errors that don't leak internal stack details
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The normalized error objects defined in feature8_missing_data_errors.json: association_not_loaded, column_not_selected, and undefined_member — each with specific keys (error, association/column/member, record where applicable, trace)

Indirect relationship loading via traversal plans ('none' and 'select') as specified in feature6_indirect_relationship_loading.json, traversing through intermediate tables using through() path notation

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect — you'll want to look at the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact expected behavior, or check the PRD sections for the relevant feature. If it's about error format, output structure, or relationship loading specifics, those are all spelled out in the feature JSON files.

feature10_record_object_behavior.json.description: Returned records support key-based field access and equality based on the represented row.

feature11_delegated_record_behavior.json.description: A returned record can delegate model-defined behavior and preserve argument and callback values.

cases.expected_output: {"value":"Foo - All about Widget A - foo - bar"}

feature1_relational_queries.json.description: Relational queries return ordered lightweight records, selected fields, first matching records, and normalized not-found errors.

cases.expected_output: {"username":"bob","sql":["root: SELECT users.* FROM users WHERE users.username = 'bob' LIMIT 1"]}

feature2_raw_sql_parameters.json.description: SQL text inputs with supported placeholder styles bind scalar and list parameters before returning records.

cases.expected_output: {"records":["Widget A","Widget B","Widget C","Widget D","Widget E","Widget F","Widget G"]}

feature3_scalar_projection.json.description: Column projections return scalar arrays or row arrays instead of record objects.

cases.expected_output: {"values":["Widget A","Widget B","Widget C","Widget D","Widget E","Widget F","Widget G"]}
{"values":[["Widget A",8],["Widget B",8],["Widget C",8],["Widget D",8],["Widget E",8],["Widget F",8],["Widget G",8]]}
{"values":[["Widget A",8],["Widget B",8],["Widget C",8],["Widget D",8],["Widget E",8],["Widget F",8],["Widget G",8]]}

feature4_batched_iteration.json.description: Large result sets can be consumed in fixed-size batches while preserving ordering, offsets, limits, and loaded relationships.

cases.expected_output: {"batch_count":3,"batches":[["Widget A","Widget B","Widget C"],["Widget D","Widget E","Widget F"],["Widget G"]],"flattened":["Widget A","Widget B","Widget C","Widget D","Widget E","Widget F","Widget G"]}

{"records":[{"name":"Widget B","category":"Foo","detail":"All about Widget B","line_items":[]},{"name":"Widget C","category":"Foo","detail":"All about Widget C","line_items":[{"amount":200,"order_total":520}]},{"name":"Widget D","category":"Bar","detail":"All about Widget D","line_items":[{"amount":300,"order_total":520}]}]}

feature5_direct_relationship_loading.jso.description: Records can include directly related one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, and join-table collections.

cases.expected_output: {"records":[{"widget":"Widget A","category":"Foo"},{"widget":"Widget B","category":"Foo"},{"widget":"Widget C","category":"Foo"},{"widget":"Widget D","category":"Bar"},{"widget":"Widget E","category":"Bar"},{"widget":"Widget F","category":"Bar"},{"widget":"Widget G","category":"Bar"}]}

{"records":[{"widget":"Widget A","detail":"All about Widget A"},{"widget":"Widget B","detail":"All about Widget B"},{"widget":"Widget C","detail":"All about Widget C"},{"widget":"Widget D","detail":"All about Widget D"},{"widget":"Widget E","detail":"All about Widget E"},{"widget":"Widget F","detail":"All about Widget F"},{"widget":"Widget G","detail":"All about Widget G"}]}

feature6_indirect_relationship_loading.j.description: Records can include relationships reached through intermediate records, with optional optimized intermediate projections and filters.

cases.expected_output: {"records":[{"customer":"Jane","categories":["Bar","Foo"]},{"customer":"Jon","categories":["Foo"]}],"sql":["root: SELECT customers.* FROM customers ORDER BY customers.name ASC","root.through(orders): SELECT orders.* FROM orders WHERE orders.customer_id IN (846114006, 980204181)","root.through(orders).through(line_items): SELECT line_items.* FROM line_items WHERE line_items.order_id IN (683130438, 834596858)","root.through(orders).through(line_items).categories: SELECT categories.* FROM categories WHERE categories.id IN (208889123, 922717355)"]}

{"records":[{"customer":"Jane","categories":["Bar","Foo"]},{"customer":"Jon","categories":["Foo"]}],"sql":["root: SELECT customers.* FROM customers ORDER BY customers.name ASC","root.through(orders): SELECT id, customer_id FROM orders WHERE orders.customer_id IN (846114006, 980204181)","root.through(orders).through(line_items): SELECT id, order_id, category_id FROM line_items WHERE line_items.order_id IN (683130438, 834596858)","root.through(orders).through(line_items).categories: SELECT categories.* FROM categories WHERE categories.id IN (208889123, 922717355)"]}

feature7_polymorphic_relationship_loadin.description: A related record slot whose target table varies per row resolves each row to the correct target record.

feature8_missing_data_errors.json.description: Accessing unloaded relationships, unselected fields, or undefined related members reports normalized black-box errors with the affected path.

feature9_value_casting.json.description: Database values are exposed with query-layer boolean status, coded status, and local-time representations.
